div.p: On June 10, 1952, Senator Richard B. Russell, Chairman of the Armed Services Committee, suggested to President Truman that a military commission of neutral nations be asked to observe treatment of prisoners held by the United Nations in Korea. The President thought the idea had merit and asked the Secretary of Defense to implement the plan. When Secretary Acheson learned of the decision over the radio, he immediately warned Truman that the invitations to the suggested neutral nations—Sweden, Switzerland, India, Pakistan, and Indonesia—would be declined. (See the memorandum by Allison, page 405.) Acheson’s appraisal was right and the scheme came to nothing. Secretary Acheson later wrote that the President’s approval of Russell’s plan was “one of the very few instances when the President did not consult me on a matter of foreign policy.” See Present at the Creation, page 655, for Acheson’s comments and Public Papers of the Presidents of the United States: Harry S. Truman, 1952–1953, pages 414–415, for Russell’s letter to Truman and the President’s memorandum to the Secretary of Defense.
